subject: ap-chemistry
theme: Acids and bases are defined by proton or electron transfer; their strength is quantified by $K_a$ and $K_b$, and buffer solutions resist pH changes via the Henderson-Hasselbalch relationship.
core_concepts: Three acid-base theories: Arrhenius (H⁺/OH⁻ in water), Brønsted-Lowry (proton donor/acceptor — broader), Lewis (electron pair acceptor/donor — broadest).
Strong acids/bases fully dissociate; weak acids/bases partially dissociate with equilibrium constant $K_a$ or $K_b$.
For a conjugate acid-base pair: $K_a \times K_b = K_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$; stronger acid has weaker conjugate base.
Buffers contain a weak acid and its conjugate base; pH = pKa when [HA] = [A⁻]; Henderson-Hasselbalch: $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log([\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}])$.
Titration curves: strong-strong gives pH 7 at equivalence; weak acid–strong base gives pH > 7 at equivalence; half-equivalence point pH = pKa.
Polyprotic acids lose protons in steps ($K_{a1} > K_{a2} > K_{a3}$); amphoteric species can act as either acid or base.

key_terms.definition: Equilibrium constant for weak acid ionization; larger $K_a$ = stronger weak acid
Solution of weak acid + conjugate base that resists pH change; most effective when pH ≈ pKa
Point in titration where moles of acid exactly equal moles of base

exam_tip: AP Chemistry titration FRQs frequently ask you to calculate pH at four specific points: before any base is added, at the half-equivalence point (pH = pKa), at the equivalence point, and after excess base.
